finding: A resource-scarcity frame reduced susceptibility to context effects on judgments; people focused on pressing needs rather than irrelevant contextual cues.
Individuals with a psychological disorder showed a deficit in voluntary suppression of aversive memories; severity of suppression failure correlated with symptom severity.
Self-promotion efforts backfired because actors overestimated recipients' positive emotional responses and underestimated annoyance, leading targets to rate actors less favorably.
High variability in a positive psychological state amplified the mortality risk associated with low mean state; the interaction showed strongest effect among low-mean, high-variability individuals.
Self-perceived expertise in a domain caused false claims of knowledge for nonexistent items in that domain; this relationship persisted even when warned about fictional items.
Identical creative output was attributed more to a man than a woman; masculine behavior enhanced creativity attribution for men but not women, mediated by agency attribution.
Explicit salience of opportunity costs at choice time increased postchoice preference spread; when opportunity costs remained implicit, the contrast effect diminished.
Communicators' use of in-group-biased language patterns cued inferences of shared social identity with targets; observers inferred identity from abstract language patterns.

Visual patterns with salient Gestalt properties were processed holistically without expertise training; weakening Gestalt salience reduced holistic processing, showing bottom-up rather than top-down route.
Threat stereotypes associated with one demographic group generalized even to young members of that group; automatic biases facilitated threat identification across age categories.
Economic insecurity caused increased physical pain and reduced pain tolerance; lack of perceived control mediated the causal link from insecurity to pain.

Parents' failure mind-sets predicted parenting practices and children's intelligence mind-sets; children perceived parents' failure frames but not intelligence frames, and perceived frames shaped children's beliefs.
Similar substitutes were more likely to evoke a negative contrast effect despite being perceived as better satisfutes; dissimilar substitutes ultimately satisfied cravings more effectively.
A bias toward inherence explanations of typical patterns led people to infer that what is typical is good and appropriate; this bias emerged early in development.
Memory-based judgments preserved a stimulus's ordinal rank from initial encoding rather than updating it; people did not adjust representations based on a changed distribution.
Knowing others understood a phenomenon raised self-rated understanding despite no personal access to explanations; effect occurred only when explanations were ostensibly accessible.
Stereotype threat and task framing reduced male advantages in perspective-taking; females underperformed in spatial conditions but not when tasks were reframed socially and contained human figures.
Power amplified ambivalence in reducing action; powerful people with mixed reactions were less likely to act than powerless people with mixed reactions.
Mental representations of a policy category carried racial and stereotypical associations; people were less supportive of aid to a person matching the stereotype-laden representation.
False signaling via moral condemnation made hypocrites judged more negatively than direct falsehoods; honest admission of misconduct eliminated negative judgment despite behavioral inconsistency.
